items.title: Always evaluate on held-out data
items.avoid: Reporting final metrics from the same rows used to fit parameters or pick hyperparameters.
items.prefer: Hold out validation for tuning; reserve test for a single unbiased estimate after choices are frozen.
items.why: Training-only evaluation hides overfitting; validation reveals generalization before deployment.
items.example: Pick tree depth by minimum val MSE; report test MSE once with the chosen depth.

items.title: Read train and validation together
items.avoid: Celebrating low training loss without checking validation.
items.prefer: Log both metrics each epoch or each hyperparameter trial; compute the gap explicitly.
items.why: The pair distinguishes underfitting (both high) from overfitting (large gap).
items.example: TensorBoard curves: train loss ↓↓↓ while val loss ↑ after epoch 8 ⇒ stop or regularize.

items.title: Use learning curves before scaling data spend
items.avoid: Buying 10× labels when the model underfits on existing data.
items.prefer: Plot val error vs training size; if both curves plateau high, fix capacity first.
items.why: Data helps variance-limited overfitting; capacity helps bias-limited underfitting.
items.example: Val MSE flat from n=1k to n=10k with high train MSE ⇒ add features, not rows.

items.title: Stop training at the validation minimum
items.avoid: Running fixed epochs because the schedule always used 100 epochs before.
items.prefer: Early stopping with patience on validation loss; restore best checkpoint.
items.why: Late epochs often overfit noise even when training loss still improves.
items.example: Best val perplexity at epoch 6; epochs 7–20 hurt test BLEU — deploy epoch-6 weights.
